html:
Leave Taking — 10 Most Likely Questions

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section A (Modern Texts). 30 marks + 4 marks AO4 (SPaG). Recommended writing time: 50 minutes. AOs: AO1 (response + textual reference) / AO2 (writer's methods) / AO3 (context) / AO4 (SPaG). NO extract — questions are essay-stems only.

How to use this document

Each question prints a typical AQA stem, a one-paragraph frame, a short technique gloss, and a skeleton plan in Sophicly's locked structure (Introduction 3 sentences / three Body Paragraphs of 7 sentences / Conclusion 4 sentences). Memorise the structure once and the same shape carries every essay. Memorise the cross-question reuse table at the end and the same scene-bank answers all ten Qs.

The seven-sentence body paragraph shape

1. Topic sentence — concept only, no techniques named.

2. Technique + evidence — one quote with multiple rhetorical techniques visible inside it.

3. Close analysis — grammar, word-class, syntax, and one further observation about the same quote.

4. Effect on audience 1 — what the audience sees, hears, feels.

5. Effect on audience 2 — what the audience recognises, registers, infers.

6. Author’s purpose — what Pinnock may be arguing through this scene.

7. Context (AO3) — one period-specific fact that lands the BP back at the controlling concept.

Memorise these once

Leave Taking argues that the diaspora’s second generation inherits a mother’s exile without inheriting her homeland — and that the cost of leave-taking is paid by the generation that was never asked. Pinnock builds the play across eight naturalistic scenes inside a North London household founded by the Windrush migration, and routes the inheritance through the female line because the male Caribbean-British figures the host country has produced have, in too many cases, been broken before they could carry it themselves. The closing image — Del cleaning Mai’s bedsit, receiving the handwritten book of Obeah practice, and reading her own mother’s palm — answers the play’s opening unbelonging with inheritance practised rather than inherited unchanged.

Key terms:

• Naturalism — staging that imitates ordinary domestic life; the play’s baseline mode, inherited from Ibsen and the Royal Court tradition.

• Cultural-inheritance ritual — Pinnock’s structural device by which Obeah practice is held inside the naturalism so the inheritance is dignified rather than folklorised.

• Patois — Jamaican Creole; the Windrush generation’s home register, set against the south-east London speech of the British-born daughters.

• Obeah — Afro-Caribbean spiritual practice carrying African religious memory through enslavement and migration; criminalised under the Jamaican Obeah Act 1898 and kept alive in the diaspora as cultural-religious continuity.

• Stage direction — non-spoken authorial instruction; Mai’s “messy” bedsit (Scene 1) and its cleaned state (Scene 8) function as the play’s clearest non-verbal statement of inheritance received.

• Episodic structure — two acts of four scenes each; each scene a separate naturalistic moment whose meaning accumulates through the sequence.

• Windrush generation — the post-war Caribbean migration to Britain (1948–1971), named after the *HMT Empire Windrush* arriving at Tilbury on 22 June 1948.

---

Q1 — Enid

Question 1 — How does Pinnock use Enid to explore ideas about the experience of the Windrush generation in Leave Taking?

Write about:

    [30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

    Frame

    Enid is Pinnock's portrait of the Windrush mother who carries a homeland she cannot return to, performs two jobs to give her British-born daughters a future she will not share, and finally breaks the eighteen-year silence that has kept her household held together but unhealed. The plan tracks three stages of the same diagnosis — Scene 1 patois discipline, Scene 4 grieving nostalgia, Scene 8 broken-speech confession.

    Key technique terms

    • Jamaican patois — the speech the Windrush generation brought with them; phoneme-level evidence of inheritance.
    • Asyndetic list — list without conjunctions ("Roast breadfruit. Callaloo").
    • Broken speech / fragmented dialogue — Pinter-derived naturalistic device for suppressed truth surfacing.
    • Stage direction — non-spoken authorial instruction; Mai's bedsit setting as Enid's only confessional space.
    • Motif — recurring image carrying thematic weight; food, weariness, the telephone from Jamaica.
    • Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses ("You tink dis is Jamaica? You tink you can…").

    INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

    • Hook (historical fact): Winsome Pinnock wrote Leave Taking at twenty-three, in the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, drawing on the North London household founded by her own Jamaican parents who arrived in the Windrush migration — and Pinnock said in 2018 that she made Enid the heroine because she had never seen a Caribbean-born hospital cleaner as the lead in a British play.
    • Building sentence (historical context): Enid belongs to the generation Britain recruited between 1948 and 1971 to fill post-war labour shortages and then declined to make space for, and Pinnock places her inside a cramped North London living room across two jobs and three decades of holding a household together while the legislative chain of the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act and the 1971 Immigration Act progressively eroded her right to belong.
    • Thesis: Across her Scene 1 patois discipline of Del in which Enid's authority is delivered in a homeland-register her British-born daughter does not share, her Scene 4 grieving nostalgia after her own mother's death in Jamaica in which the food motif carries an inheritance she cannot return to receive, and her Scene 8 broken-speech confession at Mai's bedsit in which the eighteen-year silence finally cracks, Pinnock presents Enid as the figure through whom the cost of the Windrush leave-taking becomes visible across the female line.

    BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 1): patois discipline (7 sentences — about 225 words)

    Scene focus: Enid's confrontation with Del over Del's late-night absences; Mai's bedsit setting providing the cultural register in which Enid's discipline operates.

    • Topic sentence: In the play's opening, Pinnock establishes Enid as a Windrush mother whose authority is delivered in a homeland-register her British-born daughter does not fully share, dramatising the diasporic household as a translation problem before it is a moral one.
    • Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys Jamaican patois inside anaphora — Enid's "You tink dis is Jamaica? You tink you can jus' walk outta mi 'ouse and come back any time you feel like?" (Scene 1) — combining patois (the homeland phoneme "tink" for "think", the demonstrative "dis" for "this", the contracted "mi 'ouse" for "my house" refusing the host country's standard English) with anaphora ("You tink… You tink…" twice opening successive interrogatives, the structural repetition mirroring a mother's exhausted reach for control) inside the same outburst.
    • Close analysis: The repeated demonstrative "dis" insists on a Jamaica that is no longer geographically reachable but linguistically present in every clause Enid speaks, while the contraction "mi 'ouse" compresses possession into a homeland-grammar that the British-born daughter has never had to operate inside.
    • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the generational divide in the sounds of the conversation before they hear it in the argument's content — patois on one side, south-east London speech on the other, the diaspora staged in phonemes.
    • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that Enid's discipline is being delivered in a register Del has never lived inside, and registers that the friction is the texture of a household built on a migration neither daughter remembers.
    • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the patois-versus-London-speech contrast to argue that the mother-daughter relationship in a diasporic family is in part a translation problem, and that the translation cannot be done without recognising what each side carries in the very sound of the other's speech.
    • Context (AO3): Enid belongs to the Windrush generation (1948–1971) whom Britain recruited to fill post-war labour shortages — particularly in the new National Health Service founded in July 1948 — and then declined to make space for; Pinnock writes the older generation from inside her own family's experience.

    BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 4): grieving nostalgia and inheritance redirected (7 sentences — about 230 words)

    Scene focus: Enid grieving on the sofa with Viv after the telephone has carried news of her mother's death in Jamaica; the disclosure of the saved university money.

    • Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed Enid's authority as patois discipline, Scene 4 strips that authority to its underlying cost — a Windrush mother who has worked two jobs to fund a future she cannot share, mourning a homeland mother she cannot return to bury.
    • Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys an asyndetic list inside food-motif and broken syntax — Enid's "Old time woman could cook. Roast breadfruit. Callaloo" (Scene 4) — combining asyndeton (the three Jamaican dishes set down without conjunctions, each item left alone like an unfinished sentence) with food-motif (each dish a material trace of Jamaica inside the London living room, callaloo and breadfruit standing for the cultural memory the host country has not made room for) inside the same nostalgic eruption.
    • Close analysis: The asyndetic refusal to join the items with "and" leaves each Jamaican dish standing alone like an unfinished sentence, and the unfinished syntax mirrors the unfinished mourning — a daughter who could not travel back for her own mother's funeral can only assemble the homeland in fragments of remembered food.
    • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the cost of the leave-taking Enid performed twenty years earlier as the inability to attend her own mother's funeral — grief delivered not through extended monologue but through a list of three dishes too short to carry it.
    • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the saved university money for Viv is the diasporic version of the homeland inheritance Enid herself cannot bring back — transferred from mother to daughter as the only kind of cultural memory the host country has left available.
    • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the grieving scene to argue that the Windrush mother's inheritance has been redirected — from Jamaica to London, from breadfruit and callaloo to a university place — and that the redirection is what the leave-taking actually cost.
    • Context (AO3): The 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act and the 1971 Immigration Act had, by the 1980s, made return travel for the Windrush generation legally and economically harder than it had been for the original arrivals; Enid's absence from her mother's funeral is the legislative chain made personal.

    BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — End (Scene 8): broken-speech confession (7 sentences — about 230 words)

    Scene focus: Enid arrives at Mai's cleaned bedsit as Del's first Obeah client; the eighteen-year silence about her own marriage, her own mother's reaction to her departure from Jamaica, finally begins to crack.

    • Topic sentence: Where Scene 4 had revealed the cost of the leave-taking, Scene 8 stages its possible repair — the Windrush mother finally permitted, by her trained daughter, to articulate the suppressed truths the host country had imposed on her.
    • Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys broken speech inside a stage-direction setting — Enid's halting Scene 8 confession to Del at Mai's cleaned bedsit, her sentences fragmented by "the overwhelming emotions" she has not spoken in eighteen years — combining broken speech (the unfinished clauses, the pauses, the restarts as the Pinter-derived form of cultural and personal silence beginning to break) with structural setting (the confession delivered inside Mai's room rather than Enid's living room, the cultural-inheritance space rather than the family-assimilation space) inside the same closing exchange.
    • Close analysis: The fragmentation of Enid's speech is the form of the truth's difficulty — a woman who has not spoken these sentences for eighteen years cannot now speak them whole — while the setting transfers the disclosure into the cultural-inheritance space, registering that the truth can only be received in a room the older generation built.
    • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the speech-fragmentation as the sound of a Windrush mother finally permitted, by her trained daughter, to articulate what eighteen years of host-country suppression had kept unsaid.
    • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience receives Del's subsequent palm-reading of Enid as the play's quietest reversal — the daughter who once mocked Mai's practice now using it as the room inside which her mother can speak.
    • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be addressing her 1987 audience on two registers at once — to the first-generation Windrush mothers in the auditorium, that the silences must be broken before the inheritance can be received; to the second-generation daughters, that the inheritance must be received before the silences can be broken.
    • Context (AO3): Leave Taking premiered at the Liverpool Playhouse Studio on 11 November 1987, during the cultural emergence of Black British identity as a distinct political and aesthetic category in the wake of the New Cross fire (1981) and the Brixton uprising (1981), and the play's 2018 Bush Theatre revival — opening weeks after the Guardian's reporting on the Windrush deportation scandal — confirmed Enid's Scene 8 silence as prescient.

    CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

    • Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock constructs Enid as a comprehensive portrait of the Windrush mother — patois discipline in Scene 1, grieving nostalgia and redirected inheritance in Scene 4, broken-speech confession in Scene 8 — making visible the cost the legislative chain and the cultural amnesia of the host country combined to extract.
    • Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as a translation problem, Scene 4 named as the unfinished mourning of a homeland that could not be returned to, Scene 8 began to repair through the daughter's trained reception — three stages of the same diagnosis, the doubled coming-of-age confirming that healing across the female line requires both halves of the household to move.
    • Author's purpose: Writing in 1987 inside the Royal Court Young Writers' Group, Pinnock honoured a Windrush mother who had never been the lead in a British play before, refusing the melodrama of either saint or martyr and giving the audience an ordinary woman with two jobs whose ordinariness was the indictment.
    • Ultimate moral: Enid's enduring power as a theatrical creation is that the truth she has carried becomes speakable only when the daughter she raised has trained herself to receive it — the female line carrying the inheritance the migration tried to break.

    Q2 — Del

    Question 2 — How does Pinnock present Del and her change in Leave Taking?

    Write about:

      [30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

      Frame

      Del is Pinnock's most complete dramatisation of the change the diaspora's second generation must undergo — a young woman who travels from defensive mockery, through painful recognition, to trained reception of the inheritance she had refused. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 1 stolen charm, Scene 7 kicked wall and named gift, Scene 8 cleaned bedsit and palm-reading.

      Key technique terms

      • Stage business — wordless physical action carrying meaning (the stolen charm; the kicked wall; the cleaned bedsit).
      • Recurring object — an object that returns across scenes with cumulative meaning (Mai's handwritten book of Obeah practice).
      • Anagnorisis — Aristotelian moment of recognition; Mai's "Del may have the gift of the Obeah" names Del's inheritance for her.
      • Coming-of-age / Bildungsroman — the protagonist moves from innocence through experience to maturity.
      • Cultural-inheritance ritual — the heightened device (Obeah practice) Pinnock holds inside naturalism.
      • Cyclical structure — the play's Scene 8 returns to Mai's bedsit (the Scene 1 setting), now cleaned and inherited.

      INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

      • Hook (historical fact): Pinnock won the George Devine Award in 1991 for Leave Taking — the first Black British woman to win it — and the prize honours the Royal Court tradition of new writing whose protagonists travel furthest in shortest stage time, which is exactly the journey Pinnock builds for Del across eight scenes.
      • Building sentence (historical context): Del belongs to the British-born second generation — the daughters of Windrush whose schooling was English Literature, whose workplace colleagues treat them as if they do not "speak English", and whose 1981 Britain framed Black-British identity for the first time as a distinct political and cultural category through the New Cross fire and the Brixton uprising — and Pinnock constructs her arc as the test case of whether that generation can claim what the host country taught them to refuse.
      • Thesis: Across her Scene 1 mockery of Mai's Obeah practice and her stolen charm in which the inheritance is refused, her Scene 7 kicked wall and Mai's recognition of "the gift of the Obeah" in which the inheritance is named, and her Scene 8 cleaning of Mai's bedsit, receipt of the handwritten book, and palm-reading of her own mother in which the inheritance is finally received, Pinnock presents Del as the figure through whom the diasporic second generation's coming-of-age becomes possible.

      BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 1): defensive mockery and stolen charm (7 sentences — about 220 words)

      Scene focus: Mai's Deptford bedsit; Mai conducts a clairvoyant reading for Enid; Del watches with hostile resistance and steals a charm from Mai's table as the consultation closes.

      • Topic sentence: Pinnock initially presents Del as a daughter defending herself against the inheritance her mother carries — the British-born second generation whose first instinct is to refuse what looks foreign in a country that has trained her to want only what looks British.
      • Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys stage business inside dismissive register — Del's stealing of a charm from Mai's table at the close of the consultation, framed by Del's verbal "waste of time" dismissal of the reading itself — combining stage business (the wordless theft as the physical form of the refusal Del has not yet articulated) with dismissive register (the colloquial idiom "waste of time" drawn from the south-east London speech of Del's generation, set against the patois of the older women in the room) inside the same scene.
      • Close analysis: The simultaneity of the verbal dismissal and the physical theft is the play's first quiet contradiction — Del has called the practice worthless, yet her hand has reached for one of its objects, the body acknowledging what the speech denies.
      • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels Del's refusal as itself a form of attention — the daughter who steals a charm has, at minimum, recognised which object on Mai's table is the one worth taking.
      • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the dangerous confidence of assimilation — the way a British-born daughter can call her mother's culture "superstition" in the very act of betraying that she has registered its meaning.
      • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be establishing that the inheritance Del will eventually receive has been visible to her from the opening scene, and that the journey she will take across the eight scenes is the journey from refused recognition to claimed reception.
      • Context (AO3): In 1987, second-generation Black British identity was being formed against the host country's casual racism — the school that failed to diagnose Del's dyslexia, the workplace that treated her as if she did not "speak English" — and Del's stolen charm registers the cost of forming an identity in those conditions.

      BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 7): kicked wall and named gift (7 sentences — about 235 words)

      Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Brod has just told Del the buried truth about Viv's father — "originally a loving and hard-working man who was left broken and 'empty' by the way he was treated in Britain"; Del kicks the wall; Mai recognises what the kicked wall registers.

      • Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed Del's stance as defensive refusal, Scene 7 stages her painful recognition — the daughter who had read her mother's strictness as hatred now receives the explanation she had lacked, and the older woman in the room names the inheritance the daughter has been carrying without knowing it.
      • Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys stage business inside Mai's anagnorisis — Del's kicked wall in response to Brod's disclosure, immediately followed by Mai's "Del may have the gift of the Obeah" (Scene 7) — combining stage business (the kicked wall as the physical form of a recognition that exceeds language, the body absorbing what the speech cannot yet metabolise) with anagnorisis (Mai's recognition naming Del's inheritance for her, the older woman registering in the body's reaction what the daughter herself has not yet named) inside the same exchange.
      • Close analysis: The sequencing is the argument — disclosure produces the kicked wall, the kicked wall produces Mai's recognition, the recognition produces the journey toward reception — three beats compressed into one stage moment, the play's structural hinge.
      • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the kicked wall as a release of eighteen years of Del's misreading her mother — the absent father at the centre of her childhood now explained as a man broken by Britain rather than as a man Enid drove away.
      • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Mai's naming of "the gift" as the moment the play turns — inheritance has to be recognised by an older practitioner before it can be received by a younger one, and the recognition is what permits the reception that follows in Scene 8.
      • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be arguing that the diasporic second generation's coming-of-age depends on two disclosures simultaneously — the truth about the absent father (Brod's role) and the recognition of the inheritance the daughter has been carrying (Mai's role) — and that neither alone is sufficient.
      • Context (AO3): The 1981 Brixton uprising and the New Cross fire of January 1981 named publicly what 1980s Black-British men in Britain were already living — the housing discrimination, the policing pressure, the workplace racism that produced outcomes which broke marriages and households — and Brod's disclosure about Viv's father is one such outcome made domestic.

      BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — End (Scene 8): cleaned bedsit and palm-reading (7 sentences — about 230 words)

      Scene focus: Mai's bedsit, now cleaned by Del; Mai passes the handwritten book of Obeah practice to Del; Enid arrives as Del's first client; Del reads her mother's palm.

      • Topic sentence: Where Scene 7 had named the inheritance, Scene 8 stages its full reception — Del now trained, the room now cleaned, the handwritten book now hers, and her mother seated in front of her as the first client of a practice the daughter had once mocked.
      • Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys cyclical structure inside the recurring object — the same Mai's bedsit that opened the play in Scene 1 "messy" now appears cleaned by Del, with Mai's "handwritten book" of Obeah practice transferred into Del's hands and the closing palm-reading staging Enid as Del's first client — combining cyclical structure (the unchanged room measuring the changed daughter, what looked like the end of a tradition in Scene 1 looking by Scene 8 like the survival of one) with recurring object (the handwritten book as cultural memory in material form, body-to-body transmission of what enslavement, colonial criminalisation, and migration had tried to erase) inside the same closing image.
      • Close analysis: The unchanged room is the play's quietest argument — cultural inheritance survives in rooms like Mai's bedsit when there is somebody willing to receive it — while the handwritten quality of the book insists that the transmission is body-to-body rather than abstract, the older generation's hand to the younger generation's hand.
      • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the closing image — a daughter's hand on a mother's hand, the inherited practice as the medium of touch — as a healing the play has earned across its full eight scenes rather than asserted in its closing minutes.
      • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Del's journey as the journey Pinnock wants the audience itself to take — from a default position that traditional Caribbean spiritual practice is foreign superstition to a position of recognising it as serious cultural inheritance worth claiming.
      • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be making her clearest argument here — that healing across the diasporic mother-daughter line requires the trained daughter to make a room (Mai's cleaned bedsit, Del's reading hand) inside which the mother's silenced truths can finally be spoken aloud.
      • Context (AO3): Obeah was criminalised in Jamaica under the 1898 Obeah Act — the statute remains on the Jamaican books to this day — and the practice survived through clandestine transmission across the diaspora; Del's receipt of Mai's handwritten book is one such transmission staged.

      CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

      • Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock traces Del's journey from defensive mockery, through painful recognition, to trained reception — the diasporic second generation's coming-of-age in dramatic miniature.
      • Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as refused inheritance, Scene 7 named as recognised inheritance, Scene 8 confirmed as received inheritance — three stages of the same arc, the consistency of the journey across the eight scenes confirming that change in the second generation is real and that the cost of leave-taking can begin to be paid back.
      • Author's purpose: Writing for a 1987 audience inside the cultural emergence of Black British identity, Pinnock embodies in Del her conviction that the second generation can claim what the host country taught them to refuse, and that the claiming is the precondition for healing across the female line.
      • Ultimate moral: Del's enduring power as a theatrical creation is that her arc is the audience's arc — the daughter who once mocked the inheritance has, by the close, become the room inside which the mother can finally speak.

      Q3 — Viv

      Question 3 — How does Pinnock present Viv in Leave Taking?

      Write about:

        [30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

        Frame

        Viv is Del's foil and Pinnock's second route to claiming the inheritance — the studious sixteen-year-old GCSE student whose assimilation through education looks at first like the opposite of Del's Obeah training, but whose Scene 8 decision to study Black Studies at university converges with Del's path on the same horizon. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 mediating audience-surrogate, Scene 5 missed exam and divergence, Scene 8 university and convergence.

        Key technique terms

        • Foil character — character whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Viv to Del).
        • Audience surrogate — character who asks the questions the audience is asking (Viv's "And what if I fail my exams?").
        • Off-stage event — event that shapes the action without being staged (the missed English Literature exam).
        • Code-switching (analytical category) — Viv negotiates between patois household and standard-English school.
        • Structural convergence — two character arcs that look divergent but meet at the close (Viv's Black Studies, Del's Obeah).
        • Stage direction — Viv's revision in the cramped living room as silent characterisation.

        INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

        • Hook (historical fact): Pinnock herself sat A-levels in the early 1980s in a North London household founded by Jamaican parents — and like Viv she went on to university, eventually completing a PhD on Black British playwriting at Goldsmiths — making Viv the character closest to the author's own teenage self in the play's three-generation household.
        • Building sentence (historical context): Viv's GCSE-equivalent English Literature exam sits inside the literary canon Pinnock has herself described in interview as one in which she "could not find a trace of" her experience, and the missed exam in Scene 5 is the play's most pointed structural indictment of an education system that treated Caribbean-British students as if their inheritance did not count.
        • Thesis: Across her Scene 2 mediating role between the older generation's patois and the younger generation's south-east London speech in which Viv functions as Pinnock's audience-surrogate, her Scene 5 missed English Literature exam in which Viv's path appears to diverge from Del's, and her Scene 8 announcement that she has chosen to study Black Studies at university in which the two daughters' routes converge, Pinnock presents Viv as the second route to claiming the diasporic inheritance — formal-educational where Del's is body-to-body.

        BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 2): mediating audience-surrogate (7 sentences — about 220 words)

        Scene focus: Enid's living room; the pastor visits; Brod arrives and tells Gullyman's story; Viv mediates between the older generation's patois and her own south-east London speech.

        • Topic sentence: In the play's opening movement, Pinnock establishes Viv as the mediating second-generation daughter — careful, studious, the family's hope for upward mobility — and uses her as the audience-surrogate through whom the household becomes legible.
        • Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys audience-surrogate questioning inside foil-contrast — Viv's careful mediating presence in Scene 2, audible in lines such as "And what if I fail my exams? What'll she do then?" (Scene 2) — combining audience-surrogate (Viv asking on the audience's behalf the question they want to ask of the play, what is the cost if assimilation does not work?) with foil-contrast (Viv's careful register set against Del's sullen sarcasm, the two daughters of the same household responding to identical pressures with opposite strategies) inside the same scene.
        • Close analysis: The conditional "if I fail" registers a sixteen-year-old who has internalised the host country's verdict in advance — assimilation through education is presented as the gamble on which the family's future has been staked, and Viv has already started counting the cost.
        • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears Viv as the household's careful interpreter — the daughter who reads, who studies, who carries the older generation's hopes into the school the host country has provided.
        • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Viv as the formal-educational route the family has bet on — and registers the precarity of betting on it inside an exam system whose canon will not contain her.
        • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Viv to ask the audience the question the audience needs to ask of the play — what if assimilation through education does not work, and what is the household supposed to fall back on if it fails?
        • Context (AO3): Education was the principal route of upward mobility offered to Caribbean-British young people in 1980s Britain, and the gap between the canon they were examined on and the inheritance they came from was, for Pinnock and her generation, the central friction of teenage life inside a Windrush household.

        BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 5): the missed English Literature exam (7 sentences — about 230 words)

        Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Del visibly pregnant; Viv has come to find her sister; the off-stage English Literature exam is being sat without her; Del pushes Viv out of the room.

        • Topic sentence: Where Scene 2 had installed Viv as the family's careful interpreter, Scene 5 stages the cost of her route — the missed English Literature exam dramatising the system's refusal to make space for the inheritance Viv carries, even as the daughter herself remains willing to do everything the system asks.
        • Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys the off-stage event inside Del's eviction — Viv's missed English Literature exam (Scene 5) framed by Del's "You should not ruin your chances" refusal to let Viv stay in the bedsit — combining off-stage event (the exam taking place in a room the play declines to stage, the institution kept off-stage like every other antagonistic British space) with Del's protective eviction (the older sister refusing to let the younger sacrifice the route the family has bet on, even at the cost of the bedsit refuge) inside the same scene.
        • Close analysis: The exam's off-stage status is part of the argument — Pinnock declines to stage the institution that examines Caribbean-British students on a canon in which they cannot find themselves, and the absence is itself the indictment, the same withholding that keeps Enid's hospital cleaning, Del's racist workplace, and Brod's Home Office threat off-stage.
        • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the cost of the missed exam not as Viv's individual failure but as the system's accumulated pressure on a sixteen-year-old whose family has bet everything on this single afternoon.
        • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Del's eviction as the older sister's protective version of love — refusing to let her sister's route be ruined by association with hers, the divergence between the two daughters' paths now structural.
        • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the missed exam to argue that the assimilation route is far more precarious than the family's investment in it admits — and that the precarity is produced not by the daughter's failures but by an institution that has never made room for the inheritance she carries.
        • Context (AO3): In 1980s Britain, Caribbean-British school students were disproportionately under-served by the school system — the gap between the GCSE English Literature canon and the literature their families had brought with them produced the Black British literary movement (Caryl Phillips's The Final Passage, 1985) Pinnock herself was part of.

        BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — End (Scene 8): Black Studies at university and convergence (7 sentences — about 230 words)

        Scene focus: Scene 8; Viv announces she has chosen to study Black Studies at university; the route that looked divergent from Del's Obeah training meets it on the same horizon.

        • Topic sentence: Where Scene 5 had revealed the cost of Viv's route, Scene 8 confirms its eventual destination — the studious daughter who had been examined on a literature canon in which she could find no trace of herself now chooses to study, at university, the inheritance the family has been carrying all along.
        • Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys structural convergence inside the play's closing scene — Viv's Scene 8 announcement that she will study Black Studies at university framed alongside Del's receipt of Mai's handwritten book in the same scene — combining structural convergence (the two daughters' routes meeting at the close, formal-educational claiming on Viv's side, body-to-body claiming on Del's) with cyclical structure (the inheritance returning to both daughters by Scene 8 in two complementary forms) inside the same closing image.
        • Close analysis: The naming of "Black Studies" in Viv's announcement is the formal-educational version of the cultural inheritance Mai carries in handwritten form — the same cultural memory entering the daughter's life through the academy in Viv's case and through the bedsit in Del's, the play arguing that two routes are open and that both lead home.

          Q5 — Brod

          Write about:

            [30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

            Frame

            Brod is the play's male Windrush figure — Enid's childhood friend from Jamaica, unmarried, lonely, alcoholic, a frequent visitor to her living room — and Pinnock uses him to register what happened to Caribbean-British men whom the host country failed to make space for. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 2 loose tie and Gullyman's story, Scene 7 trickster disclosure of the truth about Viv's father, Scene 8 quiet withdrawal as the inheritance passes to the daughters.

            Key technique terms

            • Mirror character — Brod as Enid's mirror (same generation, opposite outcome) and Del's shadow (the unreceived-inheritance path).

            INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

            BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 2): loose tie and Gullyman's story (7 sentences — about 220 words)

            Scene focus: Enid's living room; the pastor has visited; Brod arrives with his tie hanging loose and his clothes old; he tells Gullyman's story; the Home Office threat to his right to remain surfaces.

            • Topic sentence: In the play's second scene, Pinnock establishes Brod as the male Windrush figure whose body and dress register what the host country has cost his generation — the loose tie, the old clothes, and the off-stage Home Office threat all silently characterising a man whose right to belong is being eroded around him.

            • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels Brod's loneliness as the texture of his entrance — the man who has nowhere else to go visiting the household of the woman he has known since childhood.
            • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Gullyman's off-stage story as the structural mirror of Brod's on-stage one — the play's off-stage spaces telling the audience what the on-stage spaces have been spared by margin.
            • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Brod's Scene 2 entrance to argue that the male Windrush experience is the experience the host country has been most successful at breaking — and that Brod's alcoholism and Gullyman's breakdown are points on the same spectrum.
            • Context (AO3): The legislative chain of the 1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act, the 1971 Immigration Act, and the 1981 British Nationality Act had, by Scene 2's 1987 setting, made even Windrush arrivals' right to remain progressively conditional — the same chain whose later iterations produced the 2018 deportation scandal Brod's Scene 2 Home Office threat anticipates.

            BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 7): trickster disclosure about Viv's father (7 sentences — about 230 words)

            Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Brod arrives drunk, complains about being denied rice and peas, and inside the disorder tells Del the buried truth about Viv's father.

            • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the play's hardest truth has been entrusted to its drunkest character, and registers Pinnock's argument that disclosure inside disorder is the only register inside which suppressed family history can finally travel.
            • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Brod's disclosure to argue that the male Windrush experience produced outcomes (Viv's father's violence and absence; Brod's alcoholism; Gullyman's breakdown) that broke the family roles the host country had asked Caribbean-British men to fulfil, and that the daughters of those families have grown up with explanations they have never been given.

            • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels Brod's absence from the closing image as quietly poignant — the character who has been the play's most constant visitor receding from the room at the moment the room finally heals.
            • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Brod as Del's shadow — the second-generation outcome the play warns against — and registers that Mai's recognition of Del's "gift" in Scene 7 was the moment that tipped Del away from Brod's path.
            • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Brod's withdrawal to argue that the diasporic inheritance, in 1980s Britain, has had to be carried by the female line because the male Windrush figures the host country produced have, in too many cases, been broken before they could carry it themselves.
            • Context (AO3): The off-stage figures of Gullyman (madness), Viv's father (violence and absence), and the on-stage figure of Brod (alcoholism and loneliness) together form the play's structural argument that 1980s Britain produced outcomes for Caribbean-British men which prevented the inheritance from travelling through the male line, and which left the female line to carry it instead.

            CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

            • Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock constructs Brod as the male Windrush figure whose body, disclosure, and withdrawal together stage what the host country cost Caribbean-British men — Scene 2 loose tie and Gullyman's story, Scene 7 trickster disclosure about Viv's father, Scene 8 quiet withdrawal as the inheritance passes to the daughters.
            • Controlling concept: What Scene 2 installed as the body of the broken Windrush man, Scene 7 named through the disclosure of the broken father, Scene 8 confirmed by the withdrawal that lets the female line carry the inheritance — three stages of the same diagnosis, the male path the play warns against and the female path the play hopes for.
            • Author's purpose: Pinnock honours Brod by giving him the play's hardest truth to deliver and then letting him withdraw — refusing both the saint and the cautionary tale, treating Brod as a man whose body has carried what the host country handed him and whose disclosure has earned him the right to recede.
            • Ultimate moral: Brod's enduring power as a theatrical creation is that he is the play's quietest indictment — the male Windrush figure whose breakage is not addressed by any character and whose disclosure makes the daughters' coming-of-age possible.

                    • Close analysis: The handwritten quality of the book is the play's argument that the religion travels by hand-to-hand transmission rather than by print culture — the same hand-to-hand transmission that carried the practice through the colonial criminalisation and across the Atlantic, and that now reaches Del in a cleaned south-London bedsit.
                    • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the closing palm-reading as the religion arriving in its next register — the inheritance no longer something Mai alone holds but something the second generation has begun to perform.
                    • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the religion's survival has depended not on protection but on transmission — and that Mai's quiet withdrawal at the close is the dignified form of trust the moment of transmission requires.
                    • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be making her clearest argument about Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance here — that it is a religion which survives by being received and passed on, and that its 1980s survival in 1980s Britain is the work of older practitioners willing to recognise the next generation and trust the transmission.
                    • Context (AO3): Cultural theorist Paul Gilroy, in The Black Atlantic (1993), described the Black-Atlantic identity as the inheritance of African religious practice carried through the Middle Passage and the diaspora; Mai's handwritten book is one such inheritance staged in 1987 in dramatic miniature.

                    CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

                    • Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance as a religion that survives by being passed on — Scene 1 weary practice in the messy bedsit, Scene 6 desperate consultation as the only therapeutic resource available, Scene 8 transmission of the handwritten book and the closing palm-reading.
                    • Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as living but precarious inheritance, Scene 6 named as the therapeutic resource the host country had refused to provide, Scene 8 confirmed as completed transmission — three stages of the same religion, treated with the same naturalistic respect Pinnock gives the host country's Christianity.
                    • Author's purpose: Writing inside a 1987 cultural moment that had inherited the colonial criminalisation of Afro-Caribbean spiritual practice, Pinnock makes Mai's Obeah the dignified religion the play centres — refusing both the exoticising lens of colonial fascination and the dismissive lens of rationalist contempt.
                    • Ultimate moral: A religion survives not because it is protected but because it is recognised, received, and passed on — and Mai's quiet transmission of the handwritten book to Del is the religion's survival staged as one small physical action.

                    Q10 — Truth-telling and family secrets

                    Question 10 — How does Pinnock present the importance of truth-telling in Leave Taking?

                    Write about:

                      [30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

                      Frame

                      Truth-telling is the play's quietest argument — Enid's eighteen-year silence about her own marriage and her own mother's reaction to her departure from Jamaica, the absent father at the centre of Del's childhood, and the secrets the host country has trained the diasporic household to keep. The plan tracks three stages — Scene 1 the silences installed, Scene 7 Brod's drunken disclosure to Del about Viv's father, Scene 8 Enid's broken-speech confession at Mai's cleaned bedsit.

                      Key technique terms

                      • Suppressed exposition — the silences installed in Scene 1 and only released in Scene 7 / Scene 8.
                      • Trickster of disclosure — Brod as the character who delivers the play's hardest truth inside disorder.
                      • Broken speech — Enid's Scene 8 confession.
                      • Cultural-inheritance ritual — the palm-reading as the room inside which truth becomes speakable.
                      • Off-stage character — Viv's father, Enid's mother in Jamaica, Gullyman as the silenced background.
                      • Apposition (close analysis) — Brod's compound naming of the broken father.

                      INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

                      • Hook (historical fact): The 2018 Windrush deportation scandal — exposed by the Guardian's reporting in April 2018 — revealed that hundreds of Windrush-generation British citizens had been wrongfully detained or deported by the Home Office, the documentation of their right to remain having been deliberately destroyed; the scandal made the silences of Leave Taking, written thirty-one years earlier, read as prescient.
                      • Building sentence (historical context): Pinnock places her household around an absence — Del and Viv's father, broken by Britain and absent from every scene — and structures the play as the slow release of the suppressed truths that had been holding the household together but unhealed, the silences functioning as the diasporic version of the public secrets the host country has been keeping at the same time.
                      • Thesis: Across Scene 1 in which the silences are installed as the texture of every confrontation, Scene 7 in which Brod delivers the buried truth about Viv's father inside the disorder of his drunken visit, and Scene 8 in which Enid's broken-speech confession at Mai's cleaned bedsit finally cracks the eighteen-year silence, Pinnock dramatises truth-telling as the precondition for healing across the diasporic mother-daughter line.

                      BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Beginning (Scene 1): the silences installed (7 sentences — about 215 words)

                      Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Enid's patois rebuke of Del; the silences about Del and Viv's father, about Enid's marriage, about Enid's own mother in Jamaica are all already present in what is not being said.

                      • Topic sentence: In the play's opening, Pinnock installs the silences before installing the conflict — the absent father, the unspoken marriage, the dying off-stage mother all already present in the household as what cannot yet be spoken.
                      • Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys suppressed exposition inside Scene 1 setting — Enid's patois rebuke of Del framed by everything the household is not naming: the absent father, the marriage Enid does not discuss, the Jamaica that is no longer reachable — combining suppressed exposition (the play's central truths held off-stage and out of speech, the household built on what is not being said) with the consultation setting (Mai's bedsit as the only room in the play where the older generation will eventually be permitted to speak the truths the rest of the household has been silent about) inside the same opening.
                      • Close analysis: The silences are part of the architecture rather than gaps in it — the play has been built around a hole, and the hole is what the next seven scenes will spend their time slowly filling.
                      • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the absence of Del and Viv's father as the play's quiet centre — the unfilled chair around which every Scene 1 line of dialogue arranges itself.
                      • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the household has been built on silences the host country has helped to install — the broken father not named, the racism that broke him not described, the Caribbean inheritance not invoked.
                      • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using the Scene 1 silences to argue that the diasporic household in 1980s Britain has been structured around the truths the host country has trained it to keep, and that the play's argument will be the slow release of those truths.
                      • Context (AO3): Caribbean migration to Britain produced households in which the male Windrush figures' breakage was, for many families, a private fact rarely spoken about — the social stigma combined with the host country's silence about the conditions that produced the breakage created exactly the suppressed-exposition shape Pinnock stages in Scene 1.

                      BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Middle (Scene 7): Brod's drunken disclosure to Del (7 sentences — about 230 words)

                      Scene focus: Mai's bedsit; Brod arrives drunk, complaining about being denied rice and peas; inside the disorder he tells Del the truth about Viv's father.

                      • Topic sentence: Where Scene 1 had installed the silences as the play's architecture, Scene 7 stages their first crack — the play's hardest truth entrusted to the play's drunkest character, the trickster of disclosure delivering inside disorder what no other register has been able to carry.
                      • Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys the trickster function inside apposition — Brod's Scene 7 disclosure to Del that Viv's father was "originally a loving and hard-working man who was left broken and 'empty' by the way he was treated in Britain" — combining trickster function (the truth delivered by a drunken visitor whose disorder is the only register inside which the play's hardest disclosure can travel) with apposition (the compound "loving and hard-working" set against "broken and 'empty'", two parallel pairs naming what Britain inherited and what Britain returned) inside the same exchange.
                      • Close analysis: The compound "left broken and 'empty'" is the play's most precise naming of the male Windrush outcome — the past participle "left" registering British racism as the agent and Viv's father as the recipient — while the quoted "empty" (in Pinnock's own quotation marks) returns the diagnosis to the man's own vocabulary, registering interiority rather than imposing it.
                      • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the disclosure as a release of eighteen years of Del's misreading — the absent father at the centre of her childhood now explained as a man broken by Britain rather than as a man Enid drove away.
                      • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the play has entrusted its hardest truth to its drunkest character, and registers Pinnock's argument that disclosure inside disorder is the only register inside which suppressed family history can finally travel.
                      • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Brod's disclosure to argue that the diasporic household's silences have been kept by the older generation out of protection rather than malice — and that the daughters of the household have grown up with explanations they had never been given.
                      • Context (AO3): The 1981 Brixton uprising and the New Cross fire of January 1981 named publicly what 1980s Black-British men in Britain were already living — the housing discrimination, the policing pressure, the workplace racism that produced outcomes which broke marriages and households — and Brod's disclosure about Viv's father is one such outcome made domestic.

                      BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — End (Scene 8): Enid's broken-speech confession (7 sentences — about 230 words)

                      Scene focus: Mai's cleaned bedsit; Enid arrives as Del's first client; the eighteen-year silence about her own marriage and her own mother's reaction to her departure from Jamaica finally breaks.

                      • Topic sentence: Where Scene 7 had cracked the silence about Viv's father, Scene 8 cracks the silence about Enid's own marriage and her own mother — the older generation finally able to speak the suppressed truths inside the room the trained daughter has built.
                      • Technique + evidence: Pinnock deploys broken speech inside cultural-inheritance ritual — Enid's Scene 8 halting confession to Del at Mai's cleaned bedsit, framed by the closing palm-reading — combining broken speech (the unfinished clauses, the pauses, the restarts as the form of cultural and personal silence beginning to break) with cultural-inheritance ritual (the palm-reading as the room inside which truth becomes speakable, the inherited practice providing the holding space the host country had refused to provide) inside the same closing exchange.
                      • Close analysis: The fragmentation is the form of the truth's difficulty — a woman who has not spoken these sentences for eighteen years cannot now speak them whole — while the setting transfers the disclosure into Mai's cultural-inheritance space, registering that the truth can only be received in a room the older generation built and the younger generation has cleaned.
                      • Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the Scene 8 fragmentation as the sound of a Windrush mother finally permitted, by her trained daughter, to articulate what eighteen years of host-country suppression had kept unsaid.
                      • Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience receives Del's palm-reading of Enid as the play's quietest reversal — the daughter who once mocked Mai's practice now using it as the room inside which her mother can speak.
                      • Author's purpose: Pinnock may be using Enid's confession to argue that truth-telling is the precondition for healing in the diasporic household — that the silences the host country trained the older generation to keep can only be broken when the younger generation has trained itself to receive what the older generation has been carrying.
                      • Context (AO3): Leave Taking premiered at the Liverpool Playhouse Studio on 11 November 1987 and was revived at the Bush Theatre in 2018 with Sarah Niles as Enid — the revival opening weeks after the Guardian's reporting on the Windrush deportation scandal, Enid's broken-speech confession registering anew as the diasporic version of the public truth-telling the scandal forced.

                      CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

                      • Restated thesis: In conclusion, Pinnock dramatises truth-telling as the precondition for healing across the diasporic mother-daughter line — Scene 1 silences installed as the play's architecture, Scene 7 Brod's drunken disclosure of the truth about Viv's father, Scene 8 Enid's broken-speech confession at Mai's cleaned bedsit.
                      • Controlling concept: What Scene 1 installed as the household's silences, Scene 7 cracked through the trickster's drunken disclosure, Scene 8 began to release through the older generation's broken-speech confession — three stages of the same truth-telling, the silences that the host country had trained the household to keep finally speakable inside the inherited practice.
                      • Author's purpose: Writing in 1987 about the diasporic household's silences and watching her play return to the Bush Theatre in 2018 inside the Windrush deportation scandal, Pinnock argues that healing across the diasporic line requires both the older generation's confession and the younger generation's reception in the same room.
                      • Ultimate moral: Truth-telling in the diaspora is not the moment of a single brave declaration but the slow construction of the room inside which the older generation can finally be heard — and Mai's cleaned bedsit, with Del's reading hand on Enid's palm, is that room built across eight scenes.

                      The same eight scenes answer all ten questions. Memorise this scene-bank once.

                      • Scene 1 — Mai’s messy bedsit: Enid’s reading; Del’s stolen charm; rooster crowing. Lands Q1, Q2, Q4, Q6, Q7, Q8, Q9, Q10.

                      Three context factors that travel across all 10 themes

                      • The Windrush generation (1948–1971) and the legislative chain that followed (1962 Commonwealth Immigrants Act / 1971 Immigration Act / 1981 British Nationality Act / 2018 Windrush deportation scandal). Best fit: Q1 Enid, Q5 Brod, Q6 Identity, Q7 Mother-Daughter, Q8 Generational Conflict, Q10 Truth-Telling.
                      • 1980s Britain — Thatcher era (1979–1990) / 1981 New Cross fire (18 January) / 1981 Brixton uprising (10–12 April) / Black-British identity emerging as a distinct cultural category. Best fit: Q2 Del, Q3 Viv, Q5 Brod, Q6 Identity, Q8 Generational Conflict.
                      • Obeah as Afro-Caribbean spiritual inheritance — African religious memory carried through enslavement and migration; criminalised under the Jamaican Obeah Act 1898; kept alive in the diaspora as cultural-religious continuity. Best fit: Q4 Mai, Q9 Religion, Q2 Del (reception arc), Q6 Identity, Q10 Truth-Telling.

                      Two critical voices that travel across all 10 themes

                      • Stuart Hall, Cultural Identity and Diaspora (1990). Argues that diasporic identity is “a matter of ‘becoming’ as well as of ‘being’” — a practice to be performed rather than a fixed state. Best fit: Q1 Enid, Q2 Del, Q6 Identity, Q7 Mother-Daughter, Q9 Religion.
                      • Lynette Goddard, Staging Black Feminisms (2007). Reads Pinnock’s generation as the founding move of Black British women’s playwriting. Best fit: Q1 Enid, Q3 Viv, Q4 Mai, Q7 Mother-Daughter, Q8 Generational Conflict.
                      • For the religion / Obeah questions specifically, Paul Gilroy, The Black Atlantic (1993) provides the strongest single AO3 anchor — his account of the inheritance of African religious practice carried through the Middle Passage describes exactly what Mai’s handwritten book is.

                      Final reminder

                      Topic sentence is concept only. The technique name belongs in sentence 2, never sentence 1. Sentence 2 is one quote with multiple techniques inside it, not three short quotes glued together. Sentence 6 names Pinnock and a tentative purpose verb (may be arguing, appears to dramatise). Sentence 7 lands one period-specific AO3 fact and threads it back to the controlling concept. Conclusion repeats the three-stage thesis, then names the controlling concept, then names the author’s purpose, then closes on the ultimate moral. Never use the analytical verb s-h-o-w-s (Sophicly bans this lazy default; replace with illustrates, portrays, demonstrates, reveals, conveys, signals, enacts, mirrors).
